video_title: Michael Strain: Why the American Economy Keeps Surprising Everyone
transcript: We have seen um stubborn inflation. We have seen a war in the Middle East that has uh substantially increased gasoline prices. We have seen an enormous amount of policy uncertainty coming from the White House. And through all of this, the American consumer continues to uh spend and continues to show remarkable resilience in the face of all of these of all of these policy headwinds. Hello and welcome to Wealthy. I'm Maggie Lake. Joining me today to discuss the outlook for the economy in the second half of 2026 is Michael Strain, director of economic policy studies at the American Enterprise Institute. Hi, Michael. thanks for being with us. >> Thank you for having me. It's great to be with you. >> So, we hit the halfway mark and a heck of a lot has happened. It feels like three years condensed into one. Um but but I think we're sort of, you know, looking ahead and evaluating where we came from. Um and what's happened so far, how would you describe the health of the US economy right now? >> You know, I think uh the the the word that comes to mind is resilient. M >> there have been uh quite a few concerns about the US economy in 2026. Even more concerns about the US economy if you go back to the pandemic um which I think really kind of is the event that begins the uh the era of of of of the economy that we're currently in. But just over the past year and a half uh we have seen um stubborn inflation. We have seen uh uh a trade war. Uh we have seen a war in the Middle East that has uh substantially increased gasoline prices. We have seen an enormous amount of policy uncertainty coming from the White House. We have seen uh an effort um by President Trump to redefine the government's relationship with uh the higher education sector. Um we have seen uh Doge has come and gone um in the in the in in President Trump's second term. And through all of this, the American consumer continues to uh spend and continues to show remarkable resilience in the face of all of these of all of these policy headwinds. And so that's that's that's the one word that comes to mind. M is this resilience based on um a a really sort of healthy dynamic diversified economy or are we kind of wily coyote off the cliff and we just don't know it yet? >> You know, things look things look pretty good to me. I mean, I I think we're in in kind of a classic virtuous uh uh cycle where we have um a strong economy that strength is relatively broadbased. Um that is uh uh resulting in low unemployment. Low unemployment means that people are seeing their incomes grow. Income growth is powering consumer spending. uh when consumers show up to to spend, businesses um want to meet that demand. And that means that businesses need to hire workers. Businesses needing to hire workers is keeping the unemployment rate low. And uh the cycle the cycle continues. I think there have been a number of of of um attempts to try and explain this strength away as resulting in you know kind of oneoff factors. Um so you know it's it's uh easy to find you know very you know very good economists and very good analysts who will argue that or who have argued over over the course of the past few months that you know job growth is really concentrated in the health care sector. Um who uh uh will argue that um you know the consumer has held up pretty well but you know high gasoline prices are going to are going to are going to uh cause problems. Um, and you know, I think I think I think these uh uh these these sorts of views are they're very reasonable and they're and they're and they're and they're grounded in in data and analysis, but you know, they haven't they haven't ultimately borne out. And the one explanation that um is left standing is that consumers are just for whatever reason uh uh really wanting to power through these headwinds. just to just to be to be specific about one example. Um we had a big increase in gasoline prices as a consequence of the war in Iran. Uh quite common to hear um analysts argue that uh as households need to spend more money on gasoline, they're going to spend less money on other stuff. Well, what happened instead? Between May of 2025 and May of 2026, we saw the savings rate drop from, you know, roughly uh 5% to 3%. And so, households said, you know, we're going to buy gas, but we're not going to stop buying other stuff. We're just going to save less of our less of our incomes. And that's been that's been a pattern that we have seen, I think, really since the pandemic. And uh consumers um in my judgment are just more resilient than they were prior to the pandemic uh where by resilient I mean um more eager to find ways to sustain their spending. And I don't think we have a super good explanation as to as to why that is, but it really does seem to be does seem to be a pattern that's that's that's held up for uh you know, not just 2026, not just President Trump's second term, but but really since the since the pandemic, we had the Fed take interest rates from 0% to uh over 5% in a short period of time. Um both of those uh both of those events, the 9% inflation, the the the 5 plus% interest rates were expected to really knock households on their backs. They didn't. We saw mortgage interest rates go from 2.5% up to close to 7%. Uh that was expected to knock uh consumers uh on their backs. That didn't. We had all the chaos in early 2025 of President Trump's first term. Um, uh, you know, the the Doge and and and and the, you know, effort to lay off a whole bunch of people and, um, you efforts to stop uh, government spending that have been appropriated by Congress. You I don't think anybody expected that to cause a recession, but people certainly expected that that that might lead households and businesses to freeze up. you know, households and businesses really powered through it. Uh, we had the second energy price shock of this period. The first came in 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine and now we have the war in Iran. Um, and uh there was an expectation that that that was really going to going to going to hurt consumers here in 2026. It didn't. We we we we had an economy that that uh uh uh went from basically an open border um in uh the US Mexico border to an economy where we had um basically no net migration. >> Uh that was expected to cause huge problems for businesses, huge problems in agriculture uh in the agriculture sector, huge problems in leisure and hospitality, huge problems in uh home construction. So far, those sectors are are continuing to uh to to power forward or or or at least, you know, have not have not led to a uh material increase in the in the unemployment rate and and uh material um uh uh slowdown in in in aggregate uh in aggregate spending in the economy. And so I think I think um when you when you kind of zoom out and take that uh remarkably take that six-year view um you know we are we are we are just showing uh remarkable resilience. >> It's so interesting. Do we um I mean are we all too bearish because we're it's the you know go online anywhere in any corner of the internet and just flooded with warnings that we're in a bubble that there's a reckoning coming that the US you know era of of US dominance is over. I mean it's full of doom. >> Yeah. I think we are too bearish for sure. um you know I think that I think I but but but I think I think in addition to being too bearish um we've just made some uh analytical mistakes and so I can you know I can talk about myself I was I was I was one of the economists who expected um a recession when the Fed raised interest rates so rapidly and so dramatically uh pushing them uh again north of 5% pushing the policy rate north of 5% % you know why why didn't that happen? Um I don't think it was because I was too bearish. I think it's because I um misunderstood that the the kind of underlying structure of the economy had changed. And in 2019 I think we were an economy that could not have sustained a 5% federal funds rate. in 2022 I think we were an economy that could and I hadn't I hadn't made that that analytical leap and and and and the reason why the you know what you know what changed well uh uh investment demand uh changed um and so the kind of equilibrium interest rate in the economy had gone up in 2022 relative to 2019 which meant that the economy could could handle uh a a much higher federal funds rate in 2022. too than it than it could have in 2019. Um I think uh I think you know a second a second area of analytical um updating that needs to happen is the relationship between consumer sentiment and consumer spending. >> Uh it is absolutely true that that that surveys of consumers show that sentiment uh is um uh in the basement. Um and uh and if you if you just looked at the level of measured consumer sentiment, what you what you would expect is that the US economy was in a pretty pretty severe recession. >> Um you know, I think I think I think a lot of, you know, very reasonable, serious, smart, uh analysts look at the consumer sentiment data and say, "Oh, you know, we're we're in for some trouble. This, you know, as you say, Maggie, this can't last." And I think I think we need to update our our understanding of what the consumer sentiment data are telling us. Um, you know, a third area of analytical updating I've I've I've I've hinted at, which is um the the willingness of consumers to find ways to sustain their spending. I think consumers are just more willing to dip into savings, to spend out of wealth, uh to to to do those sorts of things in order to sustain um the level of spending they want to they want to sustain. Uh I think they're more willing to do those things today than they were than they were prior uh to the pandemic. Um so I think I think there there there is the need to to do some some analytical updating. In addition to that, I just I do think that we're way too way too bearish. And I think um you know, there's a lot of uh kind of chaos and volatility in um not just American politics, but in but in but in politics in in in Europe uh as well. Um I think uh you know people kind of think that that should affect you know spending. It should affect equity values. It should affect um X Y or Z. And I think our intuitions are that the effect will be larger at least in the short term than than than it appears to be. Um, I think uh uh I think um uh you know people are people are just kind of feeling gloomy for for for for reasons that um I I think you are related to politics are related to to uh things that are happening in the culture. Again, there's less of a direct mapping from that onto onto economic outcomes at least at least over over the short term. um than uh than than than I think there there appears to be. Um you know a third a third a third a third a third kind of factor for bearishness where I think the mapping is more direct is um the the the the advent of artificial intelligence technologies >> uh you know that people are very worried about about this and the industry has a lot of the blame for that uh I think in the way that they've discussed these these technologies um there I think we are seeing uh a real economic impact. We are seeing very low rates of hiring from American businesses. I think a lot of that a lot of the reason businesses aren't adding to payrolls is because of uncertainty about AI. >> Hi, it's Maggie Le from Wealthon. If you'd like to stress test your portfolio or see if you're on track to meet your financial goals, you can get a free portfolio review from an adviser in our network. Just go to wealthon.com/free. you know, when we're talking about the the no hiring and when we're talking about spending out of wealth, it really depends where you sit, right? If you're a young person or you're a person who does not have assets, your reality feels very different from someone else. And I know we kind of broadly talk about this as the K economy as an explanation of this sort of disconnect maybe um or the or the um sort of wide divide that it feels like we have. Do we have an an affordability, an inflation problem or uh an inequality problem economically here in the US? >> Yeah, it's a great question. And so I've been uh you know I've been talking about kind of the economy, the consumer um and I think those are important analytical frameworks to use. Obviously, you know, there isn't a consumer there. You know, there are there are, you know, 330 billion uh people in in in in the United States. Um and uh I think the the the division that you point to between asset holding households and households without assets is really an important uh uh economic division. I think it's going to be an increasingly important political uh political division as well. Um, you know, I think I think we do have an affordability issue and I would break that down into into three components. Um, the first component is that we have consumer price inflation that is just too rapid. And uh, you know, I think I think the the the Fed under Chairman Powell um didn't see it that way. And uh the the the PAL Fed would argue that that we actually we actually didn't have uh an economy with uh inflation that was problematic. We had an economy where underlying inflation was trending back to the Fed's 2% target but was kind of bumped up by tariffs and then bumped up by uh by the war in Iran. Um uh but you know when though but I think the POW Fed's view was when those when those shocks uh dissipate um underlying inflation will continue returning to to trend. I think that's still uh the view of a lot of members of the Federal Open Market Committee. But the new chairman Kevin Worsh you know he kind of came out um his first first came out swinging his first press conference this month. We're we're recording this conversation um on June 30th. He came out he came out uh in the June in the June press conference and just said you know we we have not succeeded and that is that is my view. I think I think we have uh an a problem with underlying inflation. I think underlying inflation is around 3%. I think if anything it's accelerating rather than uh rather than decelerating. Um I think the risk uh of uh an acceleration of underlying inflation is greater than the risk of a deceleration in underlying inflation. Um and uh uh I think we haven't really seen progress on inflation in in in well over a year. Um now you know so like I would be hiking if I were if I were the Fed chairman. Um, chairman chairman Worsh kind of said like we have this problem. We want to get to 2%. We're not at 2%. We've been we we've been missing for a long time. It it you know it it begs the question, well then, you know, why why didn't the Fed hike in the June meeting? Um, and so I guess we'll have to wait to see for the July meeting whether or not whether or not um uh whether or not there's any um clarity on Chairman Wars's views and and and and how those views are are are jing with the rest of the committee. But but uh you know I think I think that is an issue that Americans are responding to. So that's one one part of the affordability of the affordability issue is that price inflation is just a lot too high. It's just much too high. A second part of the affordability issue doesn't doesn't really uh relate directly to um the rate at which prices are increasing but instead just observes that prices are a lot higher than they were just uh six years ago. And I think the American people are reacting to that. You know, the level of prices in the economy, 25% higher than it was than it was 5 years ago. Um, and uh, you know, people still have sticker shock. And the US hasn't had a period of rapid inflation since the 70s. So, you know, we had we had that rapid inflation in the 70s and then we had um 40 years of declining inflation, stable inflation. And it wasn't really until 2021 that we had an inflationary episode. And I think a lot of a lot of a lot of economists, a lot of analysts didn't quite know how people were going to react to it. Um I think I think we we you know have the view that um people experience sticker shock but over time that fades. And then the third and final component I think of the affordability issue is that there are certain goods and services that are just really expensive. And for decades the American people have been registering their concern about these goods and services. The American people are concerned about the cost of healthare. They're concerned about the cost of child care. They're concerned about the cost of housing. They're concerned about the cost of education. They have been concerned about these costs for three decades and um presidents of both parties, congresses controlled by by both parties have not have not alleviated these concerns. Uh and so I think I think it's those three things are kind of colliding together. How do you see that playing out? And what is the Trump administration doing enough to are they taking the right steps to address this and bring cost down? Can they? >> Um I I don't think they're doing uh uh the right things here. Um, you know, I think I think that uh the best thing that um that the Trump administration can do on price inflation is to leave the Fed alone. And President Trump has been very bad at leaving the Fed alone. Um and and and you know really the the the administration's job with respect to consumer price inflation is appoint good Fed governors and let them do their work and and and and so I think I think Chairman Worsh is a is a is a great pick. >> So you know step one step one they've done step two we'll see. Um, you know, certainly President Trump did not leave uh did not leave Chairman Pal alone and and and we should remember that President Trump appointed picked Chairman Pal. He was he was he was he was appointed by by President Trump um on the fact that everything's just so much more expensive. There's really nothing that the administration can do. You know, we should not want a deflation. We should not want prices to go back to where they were in 2021 or in 2019. that would be that would be a you know catastrophe. We'd have you know huge increases in in unemployment if if if we were to engineer a reduction in demand that uh that aggressive. Um and so you know kind of you know the toothpaste out of the tube there where President Trump can really make a difference is on that third bucket. um all this all this uh uh these kind of staples of middle class life that the American people have been concerned about for decades and that uh politicians in both parties have not adequately addressed. That's where President Trump can make a difference, but he's he's choosing not to focus on those issues. Um and so I think I think there is a real a real missed opportunity here. Is is trade policy helping or hurting at this point? >> Trade policy is certainly hurting. Um and so I think uh uh you know if you if you if you tax something it becomes more expensive and and the tariffs are attacks on on imports. Um and we are seeing you know the price of imports uh go up. Um we are uh you know there's there's a lot of kind of confusion about who about about about the tariffs. You know we think of the tariffs as kind of a quirky sales tax that only applies to the the goods consumers buy that are imported. >> The Trump administration said that that it was the it was foreignbased companies that would be paying that which almost everyone says is not true but >> yes they say that they say that too. that is that is that is um without any evidentiary support and I think we we you know we know from uh President Trump's first term that um American businesses and consumers you know really did pay the first term tariffs enough time has passed in the second term we know that American businesses and consumers are paying the current tariffs the the tariffs um that were put in place in in in 2025 and so that I think you know there's So there's there's really no confusion about there is there is still some confusion about whether we should think of the tariffs as a tax on consumers or a tax on businesses and and you know it's it's it's pretty even but actually the m the the the the slight majority of what the US imports every year are um uh goods that US businesses need to make stuff here >> right interary goods and Yes, exactly. And so what President Trump is is doing is he's he's raising the cost of business for US domestic uh manufacturers. And you know, sure enough, like they're registering a lot of a lot of discontent and they're not adding to payrolls and and um and so and so President Trump is is uh uh yes, consumer prices are higher than they otherwise would be. The president's tariffs are uh contributing meaningfully to measured consumer price inflation. But not just that, the president's tariffs are also raising the prices that producers that businesses face and um and and and we're seeing we're seeing that that clearly in the data. So no doubt the tariffs are are uh contributing to the affordability problem. So to to to wrap up, we have a a a very resilient economy and it sounds like you feel pretty good about where we are certainly through the second half of the year. Um if there is a risk out that out there that you're worried about, what would it be? >> Oh gosh. Um I mean I think that there are that there are you know a lot of risks. Um I think the situation in the Middle East has not been has not been resolved. Um, and I think that uh uh could could could be a a huge risk to the global economy, but also to the to the US economy. Um, if that if that situation were to were to evolve in a uh in a in a troubling uh in a troubling way. Um, you know, I a second risk I could be wrong about AI and, you know, we could all wake up 3 months from now and, you know, >> of robots. >> Wonder what on earth we were thinking. Well, I was thinking more about a big crash, but yes, I guess we could also be I guess we could also be prisoners of robots. And so, I think, you know, I think I think that's that's uh that's certainly a risk. Um, you know, I I don't think the Fed is going to hike in 2026. Um, I am I am uh, you know, out of step with uh, with the bond market in that in that view. Um, but, you know, it's it would be, you know, Chairman Worsh came out in the June meeting and said, um, you know, we've missed for five years. We need to stop missing. you know, we're gonna we're, you know, we're we're taking this seriously. And, you know, maybe he comes out in the July meeting and says, "We're hiking." And then he comes out in September and says, "We're hiking." And then he comes in October and says, "We're hiking." And, you know, I can I can imagine a monetary policy mistake happening. Uh, you know, again, I, you know, I, none of those are my base cases. Um, but, uh, my base case is no hikes in 26. My base case is the economy grows above potential in 26. and for the remainder of 2026. My base case is that we don't see a a deceleration in inflation in 2026. But, you know, those are those are three those are three uh reasonable um things to be to be worried about. >> Well, I think the optimists out there hope your base case is right and and it's and it's a delight to have a conversation with somebody who sees a glass half fall. So, appreciate your time, Michael. Thanks so much for coming on. >> Thanks for having me.
